Oxidation of alcohols in one act
Prologue: meeting the oxidants
Scene 1: the mechanism
Scene 2: primary alcohols
Scene 3: secondary alcohols
Scene 4: tertiary alcohols
Scene 5: biological oxidations
Nucleophilic acyl substitution and CA derivatives
Reactivity of CA derivatives in acyl substitution
Reactions of acid chlorides and anhydrides
Reactions of esters
Reactions of amides
Organic Chemistry 2 Lectures CHEM-C 342, Spring 2021
Week 5 Lecture Slides – Week of 2/15/2021
Instructor: Kasey J. Clear

Prologue: Meeting the Oxidants
Our tale concerns the oxidation of alcohols:
Less reactive (and organic-soluble) oxidants
PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) - effective, but toxic
PDC (pyridinium dichromate) – like PCC
Swern (DMSO + oxalyl chloride) - effective, but unapologetic
about his stench
Strong oxidants:
Chromic acid (H 2 CrO 4 ) - toxic and goes above the call of duty, often acting under aliases such as “CrO 3 ” or “Na 2 Cr 2 O 7 ” in the presence of water and H 2 SO 4
Sodium hypochlorite ( NaOCl ) – Also tends to overoxidize

Scene 1: The Mechanism (or How the Sausage Gets Made)
Oxidation of alcohols is essentially a loss of H 2
General Mechanism (using chromic acid):
Most oxidants react with the oxygen and provide an electron sink

Scene 2: Primary Alcohols
Since 1° ROH have two geminal hydrogens, they can be oxidized twice
The case of strong oxidants like chromic acid:

Reagents that can stop at the aldehyde:
PCC and PDC:
Swern oxidation:

Scene 3: Secondary Alcohols
Since 2° ROH have only one geminal hydrogen, there is no chance of overoxidation
ANY OXIDANT WILL DO
Scene 4: Tertiary Alcohols

Scene 5: Biological Oxidations
ANY OXIDANT WILL DO

Encore: Oxidation Examples
Oh C=O, how dost thou form? Let me count the ways.
(assume excess of reagents)
ANY OXIDANT WILL DO
,
DCM
H 2 O
